source: Incus Server - Introduction: Creating an Incus server: status, prerequisites, and synopsis

questions.q: What is Incus, in this book's context?
options: A fork of LXD -- a container system that gives the benefits of virtual servers in a container
A hypervisor that only runs full virtual machines
questions.why: Incus was forked from LXD; it runs containers with virtual-server-like benefits, many on one host.

questions.q: Why did Incus come to exist?
options: Canonical moved LXD under its own projects, and some lead developers resigned and forked it (Aug 2023)
A security bug forced a rewrite
questions.why: After Canonical took LXD in-house, departing lead developers forked it into Incus in August 2023.

questions.q: What happened to container images after the licensing change?
options: Linux Containers can no longer offer its images within LXD, but it hosts/supports them for Incus
questions.why: LXD's bundled images changed; Linux Containers continues hosting images for Incus users.

questions.q: Should you treat Incus snapshots/snapshot-server as your backup?
options: No -- you still need a regular backup system such as rsnapshot
questions.why: The book is explicit: this is not a traditional backup; keep a real backup system too.

questions.q: Which Rocky Linux version is the Incus server supported on?
options: Rocky Linux 9.x (tested on 9.4); it is NOT available on Rocky Linux 8 (use LXD there)
questions.why: The Incus server install targets RL9.x (tested 9.4); on RL8 use the older LXD procedure.

questions.q: For ZFS, what must be disabled, and why?
options: UEFI secure boot -- otherwise you must sign the ZFS module for it to load
questions.why: Secure boot blocks the unsigned ZFS kernel module from loading.

questions.q: The Incus project recommends BTRFS. Why does the book use ZFS instead?
options: BTRFS is not available on Rocky Linux 9.4 yet, so ZFS (or another included FS) is the option
ZFS is the only filesystem that exists
questions.why: The project recommends BTRFS, but it is not available on RL9.4, so the book uses ZFS.

questions.q: What is the recommended production topology?
options: A primary server plus a snapshot server
questions.why: The right way to run Incus in production is a primary and a snapshot server pair.
